chunk_summary: This section addresses the management of lactation-related complications, including engorgement, bacterial mastitis, and breast abscess, emphasizing milk removal, pain relief, and antibiotic use when necessary.
learning_objectives: Understand the spectrum of lactation-related breast inflammation, including engorgement, mastitis, and abscess.
Learn first-line management strategies for engorgement and ductal inflammation, including milk removal techniques.
Identify signs of bacterial mastitis and appropriate antibiotic regimens based on severity and resistance patterns.
Recognize when to escalate care, including milk culture or imaging for severe or non-responsive cases.
key_facts: Engorgement and ductal inflammation often present with localized tenderness but without systemic symptoms like fever.
First-line management includes frequent milk removal, latch correction, and gentle measures like warmth before feeds and cold packs after feeds.
Ibuprofen or paracetamol is safe for breastfeeding and aids in pain relief and milk transfer.

Antibiotics for bacterial mastitis include dicloxacillin/flucloxacillin or cephalexin 500 mg four times daily for 10–14 days; clindamycin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole may be used in specific cases.
Severe or non-responsive cases require milk culture or imaging within 24–48 hours.
Avoid trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole in infants with prematurity, neonatal hyperbilirubinemia, or G6PD deficiency.
reference_quote: The default rule is to continue breastfeeding or expressing; stopping milk removal worsens stasis. (ABM Clinical Protocol 36, 2022)
document_key_topics: Management of engorgement and ductal inflammation
Escalation of care for severe or non-responsive cases
